sentences.text: Yeah.
These were, these were the so-called Nuremberg Laws, which they were initiated in Belgium, the same as they did in Germany.
And, and in the beginning, like when it came to the beginning of 1942, they started to, to round up people.

Or they advertised-- at first, they advertised in the newspapers and on bulletin boards in the streets that they were looking for people to work in Germany, that they were going to pay them well, and they were going to give them lodgings and food.
And they were going to take good care of them.
So some people did go to Germany.
Supposedly went to Germany because the, the situation in Belgium was, was very bad.
There was no food.
Everything was rationed.
And even with the ration cards, you couldn't buy-- there were not-- there was not much food that you could get.
So you had to buy food on the black market.
But in order to do that, it took a lot of money, which we didn't have.
Also the Germans had printed money-- occupation money-- which was in circulation in Belgium, and incidentally, in all the occupied territories.
But that money had no value, really.
Nobody wanted that money because it had no backing.
That was only paper, just paper money.
That's all it was.
Was only good to buy the official ration.
But regular food you couldn't buy with it.
So as I said, food on the black market was very expensive.
So people that, that were poor that had no money went to apply for these jobs in Germany.
But soon we found out that these people that went, that went there never came back.
So the, the Germans themselves wanted to round up all the Jews.
We of course, we didn't know this in the beginning.
We didn't know their intention.
We thought that they would really send those people to Germany to work there, and because the war was going on, and all the German males were in the army, and they needed the workers.
So after we found out though that nobody was coming back and that they didn't get any mail from them, nobody applied anymore.
So what they did then, they rounded up people in the street.
What they would do, they would take combat trucks with soldiers and they would surround a whole area, like a two block area, surround it.
And then make a search, house by house, and take all the young males at first, and take them in and arrest them.
And in June, on June 13 of 1942, I was caught in such an action.
And what they did with us, they only took young men.
So at that time, I was 16, I believe.
I was 16 years old.
And they took us all, put us-- brought us to the railroad station.
And they shipped us to France.
They sent us to France to Boulogne-sur-Mer, near a town, it's called Condete.
It's spelled C-O-N-D-E-T-E.
Condete.
And over there they dropped, they dropped off some tents, large tents, and some lumber.
And they said, here, build a camp, and you're going to stay here and work here.
And the guards that supervised this camp was from the organization Todt, which was also a Nazi organization, similar to the SS.
These guys were-- they wore brown uniforms.
And they were like a, I don't know, like an army engineering corp, something.
They used to do the building and construction, like roadwork, and this kind of stuff.
Well, they supervised the work that we had to do.
So we built this camp.
We put it up very fast.
We put up tents, huge tents.
And for sleeping beds-- beds?
They were bunks, like.
We had to build three levels or four levels of, of a structure like where five people used to sleep, one next to the other.

As a matter of fact, it was so crowded in there that we had to sleep one way one head one way, and then the next person had to sleep on the opposite with their head to the feet of the other guy so that we could fit five people in there.
There were no sanitary conditions, no toilets or anything.
We had to dig ditches and put-- chop trees off and take off all the branches and put the tree over the ditch.
And that was our toilet.
It was a very bad condition.
What we had to do there, we had to build a road that goes to, to the ocean, to the water.
See, Boulogne, Calais is opposite of the English Channel.
On a clear day, we could see the cliffs of Dover.
See, this is the shortest distance between Dover and Calais.
It's the shortest distance on the, on the North Sea.
So the Nazis thought that if the Allies would invade Europe, they would come from that side.
So they built very heavy fortification up there.
They built what they called an Atlantic Wall.
The wall was-- they built-- they actually built a wall that was as thick as a whole house, solid concrete reinforced with steel, heavy bunkers all around.
The whole beach was all barbed wire.
And inside the water, the ocean, going into the ocean, was all mined.
And this wall went all the way through Holland, all the way through Belgium, and to France.
And where we were sent, we were sent to build that section by Boulogne, Calais.
And we had to build a road so that they could bring trucks with supplies up to the beaches.
We had to, we had to carry water and cement all day long, back and forth, so that they could build these fortifications.
And because the sanitary conditions were so bad, I myself and some others, we were five of us, we contracted typhoid fever.
So the, the guards that were guarding us, they got scared because they were afraid that if we had typhoid fever, we would spread the disease to them.
So they took us out and they brought us into Boulogne, into the city of Boulogne, into a regular civilian hospital where in the basement, they put up a room, they put us up in a room there with beds.
And we were, for the first time in many weeks, we were sleeping in clean beds with, with nice white sheets.
And these French doctors from the hospital, they nursed us back.
We were in the hospital for about, I would say, maybe three weeks till we got better.
And then one day those guards showed up again.
And they said to us, we have to go back, they're evacuating the camp.
They're sending everybody back to Brussels, to Belgium.
So we could hardly believe them because, you know, after we saw all these things that went on there.
But when we got back to the camp, we saw that the camp was broken up and everybody was ready to march and go on trucks.
And they brought us to the railroad station.
And they put us on a train, on a regular passenger train.
But they packed us in like sardines.
Like if there was a compartment that was made for-- was designed to seat eight people, they would stuff in there 15 or 20 people into that compartment.
And of course, no, no food, no water.
But the trip from Boulogne to Brussels takes approximately six hours.
So we finally we did-- the train actually did go into Belgium.
We saw that on the-- looking out the window, we could see that they were going into Belgian territories.
And we started to be very happy.
We thought they were going to send us back home.
But when the train arrived in Brussels, they did not let us get off the train.
As a matter of fact, they connected another train that came from [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was a huge armory that was near Brussels.

And that armory, which we didn't know at the time, we didn't know this, but they used that armory to round up all the Jews from Belgium, from all the towns from Belgium, from Brussels, from Antwerp, from Liege, Charleroi, all the towns.
And they brought them to [NON-ENGLISH] as a, a, a center where they collected all the people-- men, women, and children.
And they put another train with box cars onto our train.
And they deported us from there right into Auschwitz.
As a matter of fact, they gave us numbers.
This was the 16 transport that left [NON-ENGLISH] at that time.
And I had the number of 569 given to me.
They gave us cards.
As a matter of fact, they gave us, they gave us a card with a string around the neck with a card.
And the number was put on 569.
And they sent this train directly to Auschwitz.
Now, we, we didn't know.
We had no idea about Auschwitz.
We had never heard about such a place.
So we-- the group that came from France, all those men, that was a group of about 300 that came from France.
The others were from-- were the women and the children.
That whole transport consisted of 848 men and 94 women and 41 children.
And from this entire transport, we found out now through the official records, that only 54 men survived from the entire transport of 840 more people.
So now when the train arrived in Auschwitz, they opened the doors of the cars and everybody out.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], they yelled at us.
And right away they said that the women and the children had to go to one side.
And all the men had to go to another side.
And then we notice in the background, we noticed that there were some people that were working there.
And they looked kind of funny to us because they had their head shaven.
They had no hair.
And they were wearing these uniforms, like pajamas with stripes.
And what they were doing, they were taking the luggage off the trains, the little bit of luggage what we had from France.
That came from France, we had hardly any luggage because we came from a labor camp from France, and we had very little personal belongings.
Everything we had that was of value was already taken away from us.
But the people that came from Brussels now, from [NON-ENGLISH], they had suitcases with their personal belongings.
And all that was taken away from them.
And those people that were working there with those striped uniforms, they were taking all those suitcases and were lining them up and putting them away.
And they were trying to whisper to us.
They were saying, walk, walk.
In the beginning we couldn't figure out, what were they trying to tell us.
Walk But soon we found out what they were trying to tell us, because the SS that were there that separated all of those people, they said look, you are here in Auschwitz now.
This is a labor camp, and you're going to work here.
And everybody that can work will get food and will get lodging and will be taken care of.
Now, anybody that feels that he cannot walk now should step to the other side because the camp is about 10 minutes march away from here.
And those people that cannot walk or are tired, there will be trucks that will take them to the camp.
So now we realized what they were telling us, those people with the pajama uniforms.
They were telling us to work.
So we, of course, most of us from-- that came from France, we walked, marched to the camp.
And then we found out that those people that could not walk, they never went to the camp.
They went straight to the gas chamber.
They were all killed immediately.
Now, when we arrived in Auschwitz in the, in the camp and the gate, when we arrived by the gate, in front of the gate, we saw this huge sign over the gate.
And it said, [NON-ENGLISH], work gives freedom.
And all this, of course, put us in deep shock.
We, we from one moment to the next, they hit us with all kinds of things.
The guards were standing around with dogs and with-- they had those sticks with rubber [INAUDIBLE] like.
The hit you.
If you didn't follow instructions immediately, they hit you over the head and you fell down and it was terrible.
Well, anyway, we got inside the camp.
And they brought us to a quarantine block.
Incidentally, this was the main camp of Auschwitz.
And they put us in a quarantine block.
In the main camp of Auschwitz, they had brick buildings.
The barracks where brick.
They had two floors.
There were about 400 people downstairs and 600 people upstairs.
And they brought us in there.
And we had to, we had to undress all our clothes.
And they sent so-called barbers in there that shaved our hair, shaved it completely down, all the hair on the entire body.
And then another crew came in and put numbers on our arm.
See, I have the number 72554.
Yeah, the triangle was put on later.
In the beginning, they just put the number.
Later, they decided that they wanted to put the triangle to indicate that we were Jews.
Because there were also non-Jews in Auschwitz, and they did not have this triangle.
So then they gave us-- after they gave us the numbers, they gave us the uniforms, also the striped pajama-like uniforms.
They just gave you a shirt with a jacket and a pair of pants that's very thin and no warm, no warm underwear or anything like that.
For shoes, they gave us these wooden shoes, like the Dutch wear them.
You know, they're very uncomfortable.
If you're not used to it, you can't really walk in them.
But that's what they gave us.
And then they gave us another speech.
The so-called [NON-ENGLISH], which is the block elder, so-called, he is in charge of the block.
He gave us a speech, and he told us that we are here now in Auschwitz and we are, we have to work here, and we're going to get a ration of bread and soup.
And he told us, his remark was, the only way that you're going to get out of here is through the chimney.
That's what he said.
And it was-- we were all in total shock, of course.
I also remember that after we put on those uniforms, one fellow said to the other, you know, what do I look like?
Is there a mirror here somewhere?
So we said-- another guy said, you don't need a mirror.
Just look at me, and you will know what you look like.
We all looked alike, really.
And it was a terrible experience.
Well from there, we, we were separated into different, different areas, different work details.
At first I was working on a, on a-- each work detail, incidentally, was called a commando.
So this one, this particular commando that they sent me to was called [NON-ENGLISH], I mean the water, the water works.
We had to dig ditches for water lines.
There was a lot of construction going on around the area of Auschwitz.
They were building factories and expanding the camp.
So a lot of construction work, as I said.
But the climate in Auschwitz is very bad.
It's very cold there.
It's in Poland.
And to work outside was very, very hard.
For the-- that little bit of rations that they gave you there, which consisted of a quart-- well, there was one loaf of bread.
The bread was like square-like like in a shape.
It was one kilogram.
And that was divided into four pieces.
And each person got one quarter of that bread.
I don't think that anybody can visualize to divide such a loaf of bread.
Sometimes if these guards-- yeah, yeah, what I should say also was the administration of the camp itself was run by criminals.
The Nazis had put criminals in charge of the camp.
Those were German citizens, German people that were convicted of crimes for more than 10 years.
They had passed a law that any criminal that was convicted of a crime for more than 10 years would have to-- could not go back into society.
He has to serve out his time in a concentration camp-- I mean, after he had served the time of the prison term.
So these criminals were the ones that were running the camp.
These were the block elders, like I said.
These were the kapo that were in charge of the work detail.
Now, those guys were criminals, hardened criminals.
And those guys left their frustration out on us.
So you can imagine what they did.
So sometimes what they would do, they would take such a loaf of bread.
Would take one loaf of bread and say, here, four guys, you divide the bread into four portions.

Now, can you imagine four hungry people that came back from a work detail where they worked all day digging ditches that are starved almost to death, give them a loaf of bread without a knife to divide that bread into four parts?
Sometimes that bread was moldy.
So the one person that got the moldy part would scream and yell that he cannot eat that part.
He wants a good part of the bread.
Sometimes the bread was so fresh that it would fall apart.
You couldn't really divide it properly.
When you would break it, it would fall apart.
So these were the conditions that we had to live under.
Besides this bread, each one had a bowl.
That was your bowl.
And that bowl would give you-- every day they would give you 1 liter of so-called soup.
And also with the bread, they would give you a small portion of margarine, a tiny little slab like when you go to a restaurant and they give you those little pieces of butter.
You know, on that order like that, that was the daily ration.
And they would give you tea, which was sometimes couldn't drink it because it was so bitter.
That tea was made from herbs, because they had no regular tea.
And of course coffee was unheard of in Auschwitz and in Germany, because coffee came from Brazil.
And the war was on, there was no coffee anywhere.
So that's-- that was our ration.
So to survive under those conditions was practically impossible.
They wanted to kill us, really.
That was their aim.
They wanted to annihilate us.
The people that went straight to the gas chamber when they arrived were better off than we were at that time in the camp.
Even though they needed us to work, they were working us till we dropped, because when anybody dropped, we had to pick them up, bring them back to the camp to be counted again.
Because every day when we came back from work we had to line up in groups, each block, which was about 1,000 people.
And in Auschwitz itself were approximately 24,000 to 25,000 prisoners in that main camp.
And to count these people takes a long time.
And you had to stand at attention in rain or shine, in winter or summer.
You had to stand still in rows of tens with a space in between, just like soldiers, and had to be counted.
The dead people had to be lined up on the side so that the count had to be complete.
And if one was missing, you had to stay there for hours until they found out if somebody escaped or if somebody was hiding, till they found them.
So the conditions were really impossible.

So since there was so much building going on there, I found out that they had a [NON-ENGLISH], a school where they were teaching young people how to lay bricks, because they needed a lot of brick layers because of all this construction.
So they were taking young people and sending them to the school to learn how to lay bricks.
And right away I applied for that job because then you were inside the school.
We got the same food as the other people got, but we were protected from the elements.
So I applied for the school, and I went to this so-called [NON-ENGLISH].
And they taught us how to lay bricks, how to mix certain mortars, and how to lay-- make walls and chimneys.
Different type of construction.
This went on for about two months.
After two months, it was a big help to us, because the ones that were in the school, like I said, were much better off than the people that had to go to work and were outside.
So after two months they said, OK, now, now you know how to lay bricks, now you go to work.
So they sent us out to another work detail, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
That means it was a construction of a, of a hole that was constructed, a factory that was constructed for [INAUDIBLE], the big concern of [NON-ENGLISH].
They were building this factory.
And they put us out on this work detail.
Incidentally, every day when we marched out to work, we had to march out in the rows of fives.
And by the gate as we marched out and the, and the work detail was counted in groups, every work detail, there was a band playing.
They had musicians outside, the finest musicians in the world.
These were all prisoners from all over occupied Europe that they bought into Auschwitz.
And these musicians played violins, and flutes, clarinets.
And they played every morning as we marched out.
And every evening when the work detail came back half dead from work, they were playing march music.
